access.accessMethod: Read through the pinned CME submodule in this repository checkout.
access.entityLabel: General Corpus source XML for aha2659

entities.description: The exact XML artifact consumed by this repository's publication workflow and supplied through the pinned Kyvero-Press/CME git submodule.
entities.label: General Corpus source XML for aha2659
notes: The exact file is 208,272 bytes.
Its four DIV1 containers are two omitted-front placeholders, one poem, and one omitted-back placeholder; only the poem division contains L elements.

rights.basis: The pinned repository root contains the Unlicense, but its operative text repeatedly describes software and no path-specific statement expressly establishes that inherited U-M XML, data, or markup is within that dedication. The exact license evidence is retained, while coverage of this artifact remains unresolved and cannot override rights in the upstream encoding or edition.
rights.component: Pinned CME repository XML serialization

access.accessMethod: Use the current U-M item information page and select the poem or entire-text view.
access.entityLabel: University of Michigan CME item AHA2659.0001.001
access.format: HTML generated from the U-M encoding

rights.basis: The current U-M item states that the materials are in the public domain and displays the DPLA/RightsStatements.org No Copyright–United States designation.
rights.component: U-M digital item and displayed materials

entities.description: A 2006 uncorrected OCR encoding at TEI in Libraries Level 1. It verbally retains the Bruce title page and poem, supplies placeholders for omitted front and back matter, and links 180 page-image positions across the full carrier.
entities.label: University of Michigan CME item AHA2659.0001.001
notes: All 3,849 L elements belong to the poem; fifteen are editorial placeholder rows rather than verse wording.
The inherited N='3311' between 3407 and 3415 is an encoding typo for Bruce's printed 3411 and is retained as source evidence rather than silently corrected in the artifact.

rights.basis: The current U-M item states that the materials are in the public domain and displays the DPLA/RightsStatements.org No Copyright–United States designation.
rights.component: U-M digital item and displayed materials

bibliographic.citation: J. Douglas Bruce, ed., Le Morte Arthur, Early English Text Society, Extra Series 88 (London: Kegan Paul, Trench, Trübner & Co., 1903).
bibliographic.publisher: Kegan Paul, Trench, Trübner & Co. for the Early English Text Society
bibliographic.series: Early English Text Society, Extra Series 88
bibliographic.title: Le Morte Arthur: A Romance in Stanzas of Eight Lines

entities.description: The immediate scholarly edition behind the U-M encoding. Bruce based the text on Furnivall's 1864 edition, retained Furnivall's numbering, repeatedly collated the manuscript to correct readings, and newly arranged the poem into eight-line stanzas. The target poem is printed pp. 1–121 inside a xxx + 148-page volume with introduction, notes, glossary, and index.

identifiers.scheme: Early English Text Society Extra Series

entities.label: J. Douglas Bruce's 1903 Le Morte Arthur
notes: The header note that another EETS title is bound with the source copy describes the physical carrier and does not enlarge the work boundary.
Bruce's inherited numbering reserves 136 integer positions between 1181 and 1318, but Bruce argues that one missing leaf likely contained only 84 or 92 lines.

rights.basis: U-M states that the item is public domain/No Copyright–United States, and Internet Archive metadata marks the Bruce item NOT_IN_COPYRIGHT for the US.
rights.component: 1903 Bruce edition

bibliographic.citation: F. J. Furnivall, ed., Le Morte Arthur, with a prefatory essay on Arthur by Herbert Coleridge (London and Cambridge: Macmillan and Co., 1864).
bibliographic.title: Le Morte Arthur

entities.description: The prior edition used as Bruce's textual base. Furnivall says the text is otherwise an accurate rendering of Harley MS 2252, with which it was read twice; he expanded contractions, added punctuation and other reader aids, supplied line 1413b, and numbered across a presumed loss of one or more leaves.
entities.label: F. J. Furnivall's 1864 Le Morte Arthur
notes: The Internet Archive item's metadata incorrectly gives Bruce's 1903 title, creator, publisher, and date while its internal title page and contents are Furnivall 1864. The provider conflict is preserved rather than silently normalized.

rights.basis: The scan's Google front matter describes the book as public domain, and Internet Archive metadata marks the item NOT_IN_COPYRIGHT for the US. Internal evidence identifies the object as Furnivall 1864 despite incorrect host cataloging.
rights.component: 1864 Furnivall edition

access.accessMethod: The current catalog exposes the legacy ARK but explicitly labels its digital images currently unavailable.
access.format: Legacy British Library page-image viewer, presently unavailable
notes: Unavailable is a current service state, not evidence that manuscript images never existed or will never return.

access.accessMethod: Exact current BL catalog, legacy ARK, BL IIIF, Internet Archive shelfmark, and broad facsimile searches found no working legitimate complete public surrogate. This is a dated negative finding, not proof of permanent non-digitization.
Registered or recently renewed readers may request Harley MS 2252 before visiting; manuscripts and archives must be consulted at the Library in London.
access.contact: Quote Harley MS 2252 on the collection-item request form.

access.accessMethod: Submit the British Library digitisation request form for a quotation for full Harley MS 2252 imaging or the exact target folios.
access.contact: Request Harley MS 2252, preferably specifying full manuscript or ff. 86r–133v and asking for file and reuse terms.
access.format: Provider-supplied digital reproduction; availability and format subject to quotation

rights.basis: No current item-specific image license was located. Any new reproduction is requestable by quotation; applicable copyright, contract, credit, publication, and reuse terms must be confirmed in the order response.
rights.component: British Library-supplied manuscript photography

access.accessMethod: Ask the British Library Reference Services team whether the legacy digital surrogate can be restored or whether an existing full reproduction can be supplied.

dateStatements.label: copying of Ipomydon B and Stanzaic Morte Arthur booklets
dateStatements.notes: The whole composite manuscript was assembled and augmented by John Colyns in the early sixteenth century; that later layer does not redetermine the target poem's copying date.
dateStatements.value: second half of the fifteenth century

entities.description: The one witness listed in DIMEV 2.0-alpha.1 and called a unique copy by the current British Library and Database of Middle English Romance. The target occupies the second of two late-fifteenth-century booklets at ff. 86r–133v. A missing quire leaf between ff. 102 and 103 creates an internal textual lacuna; the poem otherwise has an opening and explicit.

holding.notes: The target is ff. 86r–133v. The manuscript was bought by John Colyns in 1517, later entered the Harley collection, and became a foundation British Museum/British Library manuscript.

entities.label: British Library Harley MS 2252, ff. 86r–133v
notes: The physical manuscript is extant even though one target leaf is missing and its legacy online images are currently unavailable.
No manuscript facsimile is contained in the cached Benson/Foster PDF.

physicalDescription.extent: Composite volume; target poem ff. 86r–133v. The catalog's quire viii is 16-1, with the second leaf missing.
physicalDescription.format: Composite commonplace book made from two Middle English literary booklets and later early-sixteenth-century additions
notes: The target was copied by two fifteenth-century scribes; Bruce places the change after f. 101v.
The British Library records English Gothic cursive scripts for the composite manuscript.
The aggregate codex contains Middle English and Latin, but the target poem is Middle English.

rights.basis: Ownership and access to a physical manuscript are separate from copyright in the medieval text and from rights or provider terms for modern photography.
rights.component: Physical manuscript carrier

rights.basis: No current item-specific image license was located. Any new reproduction is requestable by quotation; applicable copyright, contract, credit, publication, and reuse terms must be confirmed in the order response.
rights.component: British Library-supplied manuscript photography

dateStatements.label: composition
dateStatements.notes: The proposals remain attributed and unresolved.
dateStatements.value: competing estimates of c. 1350 and about the end of the fourteenth century

entities.description: The conceptual Middle English poem, distinct from its sole currently recorded physical witness and from all editorial and digital representations. The one witness preserves the beginning and ending but has an internal missing-leaf lacuna.
entities.label: Anonymous Middle English Stanzaic Morte Arthur

rights.basis: The anonymous work was composed in the fourteenth century and survives in a fifteenth-century witness; any copyright in the medieval verbal work has expired.
rights.component: Medieval verbal work

entities.description: The Old French Arthurian prose romance described by modern METS as the narrative condensed in the Stanzaic Morte Arthur. The entity represents a conceptual source work, not an identified physical exemplar; Bruce's analysis posits mediated and changing source relationships rather than direct copying of one surviving French manuscript.
entities.label: La Mort Artu

rights.basis: La Mort Artu is a medieval work; any copyright in its medieval verbal content has expired. This does not determine rights in a modern edition, translation, encoding, or facsimile, none of which is modeled as this entity.
rights.component: Medieval Old French verbal work

access.accessMethod: Follow the official METS purchase link to Medieval Institute Publications.
access.format: Print edition; current stock and price subject to publisher

rights.basis: METS states that print format is governed by the guidelines articulated in the edition. The 1994 editorial expression is modern and is not made public domain by the medieval source text; no broader open license for the print layer was identified.
rights.component: 1994 printed Benson/Foster scholarly edition apart from the separately licensed XML

bibliographic.citation: Larry D. Benson, ed., revised by Edward E. Foster, King Arthur's Death: The Middle English Stanzaic Morte Arthur and Alliterative Morte Arthure (Kalamazoo: Medieval Institute Publications, 1994).
bibliographic.title: King Arthur's Death: The Middle English Stanzaic Morte Arthur and Alliterative Morte Arthure

entities.description: A pedagogically regularized two-work edition containing Stanzaic Morte Arthur on printed pp. 11–123 and the distinct Alliterative Morte Arthure later in the volume. Benson says the stanzaic text is based mainly on Bruce and was read against a microfilm of Harley; Foster revised and updated the 1994 edition.
entities.label: Benson/Foster 1994 King Arthur's Death
notes: The text regularizes spelling and plural pronouns and adds marginal glosses; one conjectured stanzaic line is bracketed and excluded from the line numbering.
This later edition is supporting evidence, not upstream of the U-M encoding.

rights.basis: METS states that print format is governed by the guidelines articulated in the edition. The 1994 editorial expression is modern and is not made public domain by the medieval source text; no broader open license for the print layer was identified.
rights.component: 1994 printed Benson/Foster scholarly edition apart from the separately licensed XML

entities.description: The current Middle English Texts Series HTML/XML digital text derived from the Benson/Foster printed edition. entities.label: METS digital encoding of Benson/Foster 1994
notes: No METS XML deliverable was cached during this research; the accessible reader presentation and the provider's XML license establish the digital layer without equating it with the PDF.

rights.basis: METS states that all XML is licensed CC BY-NC-SA 4.0. No METS XML file was cached for this research, and the license is not transferred to the PDF, print edition, or manuscript text.

next_steps: Seek a path-specific licensing or provenance statement from the CME repository maintainers.
Check upstream U-M terms and repository history for an explicit grant covering the imported XML serialization.
Until then, retain unknown artifact-level status and do not transfer the root software license to the U-M encoding, Bruce edition, or other upstream layers.
openQuestions.question: Does the pinned CME repository's root Unlicense expressly cover the inherited U-M XML data and markup at CME/source/CME_phase_1-2/aha2659.xml?

next_steps: Check Benson's papers, editorial files, acknowledgements, and publisher archives for a reel or holding identifier.
Ask the British Library and relevant university repositories whether a consultation microfilm matching the edition survives.
Do not collapse the microfilm into the BL legacy digital ARK without documentary evidence.
openQuestions.question: What exact microfilm of Harley MS 2252 did Larry D. Benson consult, and does it survive in an identifiable repository?

next_steps: Monitor the official catalog, legacy ARK, and BL IIIF services.
Submit a reference enquiry quoting Harley MS 2252 and record 040-002048083.
If necessary, request a quotation for full-manuscript imaging or exact ff. 86r–133v.
notes: The current official state is unavailable, not a claim that no images were ever made.
openQuestions.question: Will the British Library restore a public full facsimile of Harley MS 2252, or can an existing full reproduction be supplied?

next_steps: Keep Furnivall's 136 reserved integer positions separate from Bruce's codicological estimate of 84 or 92 lost lines.
Consult Hissiger 1975, Noguchi 1990, and later codicological scholarship before adopting any reconstruction.
openQuestions.question: How many original lines and stanzas were lost with the missing Harley leaf?

reviewNotes: The cataloged work is the anonymous Stanzaic Morte Arthur, not the composite Harley commonplace book, Bruce's full EETS volume, the later two-work Benson/Foster volume, Malory's prose work, or the distinct Alliterative Morte Arthure.
Every one of the 3,849 XML L elements is a direct child of an LG under the sole BODY poem division; no L is nested in a note, title page, front matter, or back matter.

The raw 3,849 L count includes fifteen non-verbal editorial rows. Removing them leaves 3,834 textual verse entries, matching DIMEV; one of those is the bracketed supplied line 1413b, absent from Harley, so 3,833 entries transcribe extant manuscript verse.

Furnivall's main numbering jumps from 1181 to 1318, reserving 136 integer positions for the lacuna, and ends at 3969. One unnumbered gap-marker L and fifteen b/c-suffixed L entries make the terminal number exceed the raw XML count by exactly 120; the 3969 repertory figure is therefore conventional lineation, not a tag or physical-line count.

The 482 XML LG elements are Bruce's editorial stanza arrangement. Direct L sizes are 479×8, 1×7, 1×6, and 1×4; after editorial placeholders are removed, textual sizes are 471×8, 2×7, 8×6, and 1×4. Furnivall says the manuscript writes the lines continuously, while DIMEV and MERM support the eight-line stanza analysis.

The current British Library catalog dates copying of the target poem to the second half of the fifteenth century. Its early-sixteenth-century compilation, London association, 1460–1540 aggregate range, and mixed English/Latin language fields describe the composite host and are not transferred wholesale to the poem.

The cached Benson/Foster PDF is a 297-page image-only scan of a 1994 printed edition containing two distinct poems. Stanzaic Morte Arthur occupies PDF pages 16–128 / printed pp. 11–123; the Alliterative Morte Arthure begins at PDF page 134 and is outside aha2659.

The one-witness claim is bounded to the current British Library record, DIMEV 2.0-alpha.1 record 3252, and the current Database of Middle English Romance. No fragments are listed in those sources; this is not a universal guarantee against future discoveries.

Rights for the abstract medieval work, physical witness, Furnivall and Bruce editions, scans and OCR, U-M encoding, repository XML, DIMEV data, METS print/digital layers, and any British Library reproduction are distinct.

lineage.summary: The repository artifact is the pinned CME XML for University of Michigan item AHA2659.0001.001. U-M encoded J. Douglas Bruce's 1903 EETS volume as uncorrected OCR at TEI in Libraries Level 1, but the verbal XML boundary is only the title page and Stanzaic Morte Arthur on printed pp. 1–121; introduction, notes, glossary, index, and other carrier matter are represented only by omission placeholders and page links. Bruce explicitly based his text on F. J. Furnivall's 1864 edition, retained Furnivall's line numbering, and used repeated manuscript collations to correct it, so the graph records Bruce as version_of Furnivall and collated_against Harley MS 2252 rather than as a newly asserted continuous transcription. Furnivall states that his text is an accurate rendering read twice against Harley. Harley ff. 86r–133v is the one witness in current DIMEV and is called a unique copy by the British Library and Database of Middle English Romance, but a missing leaf between ff. 102 and 103 creates an internal lacuna. A later Benson/Foster edition is based mainly on Bruce and was checked against an unidentified microfilm; that unresolved reproduction remains between the later edition and the manuscript. Cached Bruce, Furnivall, DIMEV, and Benson/Foster deliverables remain distinct from the currently unavailable British Library manuscript images, and all rights and access layers are separately recorded.

catalogSummary.title: Stanzaic Morte Arthur

catalogingSubject.label: Stanzaic Morte Arthur
catalogingSubject.scope_note: The anonymous Middle English stanzaic romance conventionally titled Stanzaic Morte Arthur, represented by its sole currently recorded, internally lacunose witness in British Library Harley MS 2252, ff. 86r–133v. The subject is the medieval poem as recoverable from that witness, not Harley 2252 as a composite commonplace book, F. J. Furnivall's 1864 or J. Douglas Bruce's 1903 scholarly editions and apparatus, the whole bound EETS carrier, the later Benson/Foster volume that also contains the distinct Alliterative Morte Arthure, the University of Michigan encoding and linked page images, or the checked-in repository XML.

contentParts.label: Stanzaic Morte Arthur

locators.value: 86r–133v, with one leaf missing between 102v and 103r

notes: This is a whole-work cataloging part with an internal physical lacuna, not a claim that the sole witness preserves every original line.
The 482 LG groups are not separately cataloged as intrinsic sections.
contentParts.summary: The whole recoverable poem, from the opening appeal to listeners through Arthur's fall, the deaths of Lancelot and Guinevere, and the manuscript explicit, with an internal lost-leaf lacuna after line 1181.

assertion.notes: The Database of Middle English Romance gives c. 1350, and Benson/Foster calls a mid-fourteenth-century date a probable guess.
Bruce's older dating argument is based chiefly on language. It is retained separately rather than silently reconciled with the c. 1350 estimate.

dateStatements.display: About the end of the fourteenth century

assertion.notes: This dates the copying of the target booklet, not the poem's composition and not the early-sixteenth-century compilation of the whole commonplace book.

dateStatements.display: Second half of the fifteenth century

extent.description: One poem occupies one BODY/DIV1 and 482 direct LG TYPE='stanza' groups. All 3,849 XML L elements are direct children of those LGs; none is nested in a note, front matter, back matter, or another division. Fifteen are non-verbal editorial rows (fourteen suffix-numbered dotted/empty rows plus one unnumbered asterisk gap marker), leaving 3,834 non-placeholder verse entries, exactly the count reported in current DIMEV. One of those 3,834 is Bruce's bracketed, Furnivall-supplied line 1413b, expressly absent from the manuscript, so 3,833 entries represent extant manuscript verse. Furnivall's inherited main numbering jumps from 1181 to 1318, reserving 136 integer positions for the lost-leaf lacuna, and ends at 3969. The unnumbered gap marker makes that jump contribute 135 positions relative to raw XML order; fifteen b/c-suffixed L entries do not advance the main integer sequence, reducing the net difference to 120, so 3969 minus 3849 equals 120. Of the 482 LGs, 479 contain eight direct L children, one seven, one six, and one four; after non-verbal placeholders are excluded, the distribution is 471 groups of eight textual entries, two of seven, eight of six, and one of four. The groups follow Bruce's editorial stanza arrangement rather than manuscript layout. One inherited N='3311' between 3407 and 3415 is a source-encoding typo for printed 3411 and is not silently corrected here.

assertion.notes: Bruce newly arranged the printed text in eight-line stanzas, while Furnivall says the manuscript writes the lines continuously. The 482 XML LG elements therefore represent editorial stanza analysis, not manuscript graphic divisions.

formStatements.description: A stanzaic narrative poem in predominantly eight-line, four-beat stanzas, usually rhyming abababab, with attested irregular groups and an internal lost-leaf lacuna.

languageStatements.label: Middle English
notes: The Latin and later English material elsewhere in Harley MS 2252 belongs to the host codex, not to this poem.
The edition chain is not flattened: Bruce explicitly based his text on Furnivall while repeatedly collating Harley; Furnivall describes his text as an accurate rendering read twice against Harley.
The modern Benson/Foster edition is supporting evidence, not the source of the U-M XML, and its cached PDF is a printed-edition scan rather than manuscript photography.

Rights for the medieval work, Furnivall and Bruce editions, U-M encoding, repository XML, Internet Archive/Google derivatives, DIMEV data, METS edition, and any British Library reproduction are recorded separately in the lineage manifest.

next_steps: Review recent linguistic and textual scholarship beyond the general repertory and teaching-edition summaries.
Retain materially different proposals as attributed assertions rather than selecting an unsupported compromise.
openQuestions.question: Can the poem's date and dialect region be narrowed without collapsing the c. 1350/North Midlands and late-fourteenth-century/northern North-West Midlands proposals?

next_steps: Keep Furnivall's 136 reserved integer positions separate from Bruce's physical estimate of 84 or 92 lost lines.
Consult later critical editions and codicological work before preferring a reconstruction.
notes: Bruce rejects H. O. Sommer's proposed transposition and treats the loss as one leaf; no lost wording is supplied in this manifest.
openQuestions.question: How many original lines were lost with the missing Harley leaf?

next_steps: Monitor the official Archives and Manuscripts Catalogue and legacy ARK for restored digital content.
Use the British Library physical-item request, reference enquiry, or full-manuscript reproduction-order routes in the lineage manifest.
openQuestions.question: Will the British Library restore a complete public digital facsimile of Harley MS 2252?

assertion.notes: Both modern sources qualify the localization; it is not a proven place of composition.
This is Bruce's attributed historical proposal, not a synthesis with the modern North Midlands label.

placeStatements.placeLabel: Northern edge of the North-West Midlands, England

scope.description: 1864 edition of the poem from Harley MS 2252
1903 re-edition, based on Furnivall and repeatedly collated against Harley MS 2252

subjects.label: The fall and death of King Arthur

summaries.value: An anonymous Middle English romance in eight-line stanzas recounting the affair of Lancelot and Guinevere, the false accusation and defense of the queen, the exposure of the lovers, war between Lancelot and Arthur, Mordred's usurpation, Arthur's fatal final battle, and the deaths and burials of the principal figures. Modern scholarship describes it as a condensation and adaptation of the French La Mort Artu.

The poem is currently recorded in one substantially complete medieval witness, Harley MS 2252, ff. 86r–133v. A missing leaf between ff. 102 and 103 creates an internal lacuna, so 'unique copy' must not be expanded into a claim of physically complete survival. Current DIMEV 2.0-alpha.1 lists one witness; the British Library calls it a unique copy; the Database of Middle English Romance likewise records one unique copy and no fragments.

The U-M/General Corpus XML verbally encodes Bruce's title page and poem on printed pp. 1–121. Two front-matter placeholders, one back-matter placeholder, and page-break scaffolding refer to omitted introduction, notes, glossary, index, and other carrier pages; those linked or placeholder-only regions are not additional content parts of the medieval work.

assertion.notes: This conventional title distinguishes the poem from the Alliterative Morte Arthure and from Malory's prose Morte Darthur.

titles.supplied_by: F. J. Furnivall and J. Douglas Bruce

titles.value: Lordingis that ar leff and dere, lystenyth and I shall you tell

assertion.notes: Bruce reports that the explicit is repeated in different ink but apparently by the same hand.
